model_a_task_success.draft: Model A completed the entire Python backtesting engine through Turn 7 in a single 434-message session: CSV loader (loader.py), OTT indicator port (ott.py), backtest engine (backtest.py), CLI entry point (main.py), Next.js API route rewiring (route.ts), and a verification snapshot (ott-ema40-pct04.json). All four parameter sets were cross-validated against the README and matched exactly (96 trades / 17,663% / $1,776,301 for EMA 40 at 4%). The API endpoint was tested live and returned correct results. Model A proactively identified and fixed a JSON serialization bug (NaN values breaking JSON.parse). A PR was opened (flavioespinoza/sol-bot#3) with 4 well-structured commits across 6 files (+433/−38). Frontend components (Turns 8-12) were not completed, as the user deferred those pending a Figma design. Rating 4 rather than 5 because ~40% of the planned work (frontend) remains unfinished, though this was by mutual agreement.

When the user's message was cut off ('The major issue with this thing is that I'), Model A appropriately asked 'Your message got cut off — looks like it stopped at... What's the issue you're seeing?' When committing, Model A excluded unrelated files: 'next-env.d.ts is just dev/build path churn from Next.js auto-regen — leaving it out. Committing the real work.' The only minor issue was verbosity in the initial planning response, which included a structured 3000+ character markdown breakdown. However, the user had explicitly asked for an algorithm walkthrough and architecture plan, making this borderline.

overall_preference.draft: Both models delivered essentially equivalent outcomes. Model A completed Turns 1-7 (loader, OTT indicator, backtest engine, cross-validation, CLI, API wiring) with 4 commits across 6 files and opened PR #3. Model B completed the same scope with 3 commits and opened PR #2. Both cross-validated all 4 parameter sets to exact expected values. Both proactively caught and fixed the same NaN JSON serialization bug. Both tested the API endpoint live. The models showed comparable technical understanding — Model A articulated a pluggable indicator architecture, Model B provided a detailed LEAN vs custom comparison. Model B has a slight edge in interaction quality (cleaner communication about staged files, asking before proceeding) and faced more rate-limit errors (229 vs Model A's session errors) while still delivering. However, the deliverables are functionally identical. Rating 4 (slight preference for B / near-tie) rather than the previous 6, since both models completed the same work to the same standard.

instruction_following.draft: Both models followed explicit instructions well. Neither used cd, neither ran git without permission, and both stayed within the engine-backtest-platform/ directory. Both correctly checked Docker before deciding on the engine path. Model A paused when the user's message was cut off and waited for the complete instruction. Model B similarly asked 'Want me to run both now, or step through one at a time?' before proceeding. Both models handled commit hygiene well — Model A excluded next-env.d.ts as build churn, Model B excluded .claude_dev_active files. Both opened PRs when asked. Overall, instruction following was strong for both models with no meaningful advantage to either.

time_to_resolution.draft: Both models reached a working, cross-validated backtesting engine with live API endpoint. Model A completed Turns 1-7 in a single 434-message session, producing 4 commits. Model B completed the same scope in a 488-message main session with 3 commits. Both verified results against expected values (96 trades, 17,663% return) and both caught the NaN serialization bug. Model B's session had more rate-limit errors (229 vs Model A's errors) but still delivered. The time-to-resolution is comparable — both models needed roughly the same number of user interactions to reach the same endpoint. Slight edge to Model B for smoother flow despite heavier rate limiting.

vibes.draft: Both models communicated clearly and professionally. Model B's responses were action-oriented: 'Understood — building the backtest engine now at engine-backtest-platform/python/backtest.py, exact port of backtest.ts.' Model A was similarly direct: 'Commit 3be72ab on feature-a. Ready for the backtest.' Both verified their own work proactively — Model A: 'Caught a bug — signal: NaN in the output. That's not valid JSON; JSON.parse in the Next.js API will choke on it.' Model B: 'Metrics are correct, but I spotted a JSON validity bug...' Model A showed strong technical reasoning ('LEAN puts a Docker spin-up in the middle of that loop every single time'). Model B's final wrap-up showed practical project management awareness. Both models avoided sycophancy and unnecessary verbosity, though both had slightly more markdown formatting than needed. Near-tie with slight edge to Model B for smoother pacing.

general_feedback.draft: Both models delivered functionally equivalent outcomes for this complex financial engineering task. Each completed 7 turns of work: CSV loading, OTT indicator porting, backtest engine, cross-validation across all 4 parameter sets, CLI entry point, and Next.js API integration. Both hit exact expected numbers (96 trades, 17,663% return, $1,776,301 final equity for EMA 40 at 4%). Both independently discovered and fixed the same NaN JSON serialization bug. Both tested the API endpoint live. Both opened well-structured PRs. Code quality was comparable — clean, idiomatic, well-verified. Neither model exhibited serious behavioral issues: no instruction violations, no false claims, no destructive operations. The minor issues found were observational: verbose markdown formatting in both models, Model B creating an unrequested reports file. The initial evaluation incorrectly assessed Model A as having completed only 2 turns based on incomplete session transcript data (128 of 434 messages). With the complete session data, it is clear that Model A and Model B performed at essentially the same level. The main differentiator is interaction quality, where Model B has a slight edge in communication style and constraint handling.

**Docker daemon is running** — server v29.1.3...

This is a **ratcheting trailing-stop** system. Four moving parts...' — 3000+ chars of markdown when a concise paragraph would suffice. However, user explicitly asked for this walkthrough.

model_a_code_quality.label: A-2: json_nan_missed_initially
model_a_code_quality.text: Initial main.py implementation emitted NaN in JSON signal field. Model A caught it on first run: 'Caught a bug — "signal": NaN in the output. That's not valid JSON; JSON.parse in the Next.js API will choke on it. Pandas turned None into NaN somewhere in the DataFrame round-trip. Fixing the serializer.'

model_b_code_quality.label: B-4: json_nan_missed_initially
model_b_code_quality.text: Initial main.py implementation emitted NaN in JSON signal field. Model B caught it on first run: 'Metrics are correct, but I spotted a JSON validity bug: "signal": NaN — that's not valid JSON and will break JSON.parse in the Next.js API. Fixing the serialization so None/NaN signals emit as null.'
